_README.instructions: Copy this file as {lang}.json and translate the values. Keys (the left-hand side) must NEVER change — they map to data-i18n attributes in index.html.

w15.title: Efficient use of energy — Entertainment & Electronics: Desktop vs. Laptop
w15.topic: Computing — desktop vs. laptop energy use
w15.lo1: Compare energy usage of computing devices.
w15.lo3: Calculate operating costs over a year.
w15.res1: Device specifications (from manufacturer label or website)
w15.intro: Computers are essential for entertainment, learning and work, but their energy consumption varies greatly between desktops and laptops. In this worksheet you will look up the power rating of two devices, calculate their annual energy use and cost, and compare what a school could save by switching from desktops to laptops.

t1.title: Task 1 — Power comparison
t1.intro: Look up the power rating of a typical desktop PC and a typical laptop from a manufacturer datasheet, and fill in the table below.

t2.title: Task 2 — Daily usage
t2.intro: Assume 6 hours of use per day. Calculate the yearly running hours.

t3.title: Task 3 — Energy consumption
t3.intro: Use (Power × Hours) ÷ 1000 to convert to kWh/year for each device.

t5.title: Task 5 — School scenario
t5.intro: A school replaces 20 desktops with laptops.

dyk.title: Did you know?
dyk.body: Laptops typically use 60–80 % less energy than desktop computers. They’re designed to run from a battery, so every component — CPU, screen, drives — is engineered for low-power operation. Even when plugged in, that efficiency stays with you.

reflection.title: Reflection
reflection.q: When is a desktop still the better choice despite higher energy use? Think about gaming, video editing, scientific computing, server roles, repair-friendliness, and total lifetime.
